annotations.label: Structure (informal)

anchor.snippet: a structure consists of a set along with a collection of finitary operations and relations

annotations.note: `Structure` interprets each n-ary function symbol as `(Fin n → M) → M` and each relation as `(Fin n → M) → Prop`, matching the informal notion.

annotations.label: Model

anchor.snippet: a structure is called a model if it satisfies all the sentences of that theory

annotations.note: `Theory.Model T M` holds exactly when `M` realizes every sentence of `T`.

annotations.label: Structure (formal triple)

anchor.snippet: a structure can be defined as a triple

annotations.note: Mathlib splits the triple into domain `M`, signature `L`, and the `Structure` interpretation rather than one bundled object, but captures the same data.

annotations.label: Domain

anchor.snippet: The domain of a structure is an arbitrary set

annotations.note: The domain is represented directly by the carrier type `M` in `[L.Structure M]`, with no dedicated declaration.

annotations.label: Signature

anchor.snippet: of a structure consists of: a set

annotations.note: `Language` is a pair `Functions, Relations : ℕ → Type` giving the function and relation symbols of each arity.

annotations.label: Arity

anchor.snippet: is called the arity of

annotations.note: Arity is encoded as the natural-number index `n` in `L.Functions n`/`L.Relations n` rather than as a separate arity function.

annotations.label: Algebraic signature / algebra

anchor.snippet: a signature with no relation symbols is called an algebraic signature

annotations.note: `IsAlgebraic L` states every `L.Relations n` is empty, i.e. the signature has no relation symbols.

annotations.label: Interpretation function

anchor.snippet: assigns functions and relations to the symbols of the signature

annotations.note: The `funMap` and `RelMap` fields of `Structure` are precisely the interpretation assigning concrete functions/relations to symbols.

annotations.label: Constant symbol

anchor.snippet: is called a constant symbol

annotations.note: `L.Constants` is defined as `L.Functions 0`, the nullary (constant) function symbols, with interpretation `constantMap`.

annotations.label: Standard signature for fields

anchor.snippet: for fields consists of two binary function symbols

annotations.note: `Language.ring` has exactly the symbols `+`, `*`, `-`, `0`, `1`, which is the standard field/ring signature used for the theory of fields.

anchors.snippet: for fields consists of two binary function symbols

annotations.label: Integers as a structure

anchor.snippet: of integers , which is not a field, is also a

annotations.note: `compatibleRingOfRing` turns any ring (including `ℤ`) into a `Language.ring` structure, but `ℤ` is not given as a dedicated worked example.

annotations.label: Signature for ordered fields

anchor.snippet: A signature for ordered fields needs an additional binary relation

annotations.note: `Language.order` supplies the extra `≤` relation and `Language.sum` could combine it with `Language.ring`, but no dedicated ordered-field signature is defined.

annotations.label: Signature for set theory

anchor.snippet: The ordinary signature for set theory includes a single binary relation

annotations.note: No first-order language with a single membership relation `∈` for set theory exists in Mathlib's ModelTheory.

annotations.label: Induced substructure

anchor.snippet: is called an (induced) substructure of

annotations.note: `Substructure` is a subset closed under all function symbols, inheriting the interpretations from the ambient structure.

annotations.label: Closed subset

anchor.snippet: is called closed if it is closed under the functions of

annotations.note: `ClosedUnder f s` expresses closure under a function symbol, and `Substructure.fun_mem`/`mem_closed_iff` require closure under all of them.

annotations.label: Generated closed subset (hull)

anchor.snippet: there is a smallest closed subset of

annotations.note: `Substructure.closure L s` is the least substructure containing `s`, characterized by `closure_le` and `subset_closure`.

annotations.label: Hull is a finitary closure operator

anchor.snippet: is a finitary closure operator on the set of subsets of

annotations.note: `closure` is built as a `LowerAdjoint`/`GaloisInsertion` closure operator and `mem_closure_iff_exists_term` implies finitarity, but it is not stated as a `finitary` closure operator.

annotations.label: Closed subsets correspond to induced substructures

anchor.snippet: is a closed subset, then

annotations.note: `mem_closed_iff` shows a set is closure-closed iff closed under every function symbol, which is exactly being a substructure's carrier.

annotations.label: Substructures form a lattice

anchor.snippet: of a structure form a lattice

annotations.note: `L.Substructure M` is given a `CompleteLattice` instance, stronger than the asserted lattice.

annotations.label: Rationals, reals, complexes as substructures

anchor.snippet: the rational numbers form a substructure of the real numbers

annotations.note: The general `Substructure` framework exists but these specific `ℚ ⊆ ℝ ⊆ ℂ` field-language substructures are not worked out in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integers as a substructure

anchor.snippet: The set of integers gives an even smaller substructure of the real numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize `ℤ` as a model-theoretic substructure of `ℝ` in the field signature.

annotations.label: Graphs as structures

anchor.snippet: The most obvious way to define a graph is a structure with a signature

annotations.note: `Language.graph` has one binary relation `adj` and `SimpleGraph.structure` makes any simple graph a structure for it.

annotations.label: Homomorphism

anchor.snippet: that preserves the functions and relations

annotations.note: `Hom` (`M →[L] N`) is a map commuting with function interpretations and forward-preserving relations.

annotations.label: Category σ-Hom

anchor.snippet: there is a concrete category

annotations.note: `Hom.id`, `Hom.comp`, and `Hom.comp_assoc` give all the categorical data, but no `CategoryTheory.Category` instance on `L`-structures is registered.

annotations.label: Strong homomorphism

anchor.snippet: is sometimes called a strong homomorphism if the converse implication from above also holds

annotations.note: `StrongHomClass.map_rel` requires the relation-preservation to hold as an iff (both directions).

annotations.label: Strong homomorphisms form a subcategory

anchor.snippet: The strong homomorphisms give rise to a subcategory

annotations.note: `StrongHomClass` captures strong homomorphisms, but with no category of structures there is no formalized subcategory.

annotations.label: Embedding

anchor.snippet: is called a (σ-) embedding if it is injective and

annotations.note: `Embedding` (`M ↪[L] N`) is an injection commuting with functions and preserving relations in both directions.

annotations.label: Embedding = injective strong homomorphism

anchor.snippet: Thus an embedding is the same thing as a strong homomorphism which is injective

annotations.note: `Embedding.strongHomClass` makes embeddings strong homs and `StrongHomClass.toEmbedding` builds an embedding from an injective strong hom, giving the equivalence.

annotations.label: Induced substructures are subobjects in σ-Emb

anchor.snippet: Induced substructures correspond to subobjects in

annotations.note: `Substructure.subtype`/`inclusion` realize substructures via embeddings, but there is no category to phrase the subobject correspondence.

annotations.label: Subgraph identity is a homomorphism

anchor.snippet: the identity map id: H → G is a homomorphism

annotations.note: The general substructure `inclusion` is an embedding (hence homomorphism), but the specific subgraph inclusion as a graph-language homomorphism is not stated.

annotations.label: Homomorphism problem

anchor.snippet: of a finite relational signature, find a homomorphism

annotations.note: The computational homomorphism decision problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: CSP translates to homomorphism problem

anchor.snippet: Every constraint satisfaction problem (CSP) has a translation into the homomorphism problem

annotations.note: Constraint satisfaction problems and their reduction to the homomorphism problem are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conjunctive query equivalent to homomorphism problem

anchor.snippet: the conjunctive query problem is also equivalent to the homomorphism problem

annotations.note: Database conjunctive query evaluation and its equivalence to the homomorphism problem are not formalized.

annotations.label: Satisfaction relation

anchor.snippet: defined for all formulas

annotations.note: `BoundedFormula.Realize` (with `Formula.Realize`/`Sentence.Realize`, notation `M ⊨ φ`) is the inductively defined satisfaction relation.

annotations.label: Model of a theory

anchor.snippet: is said to be a model of a theory

annotations.note: `Theory.Model T M` (notation `M ⊨ T`) holds iff `M` satisfies every sentence of `T`.

annotations.label: Definable relation

anchor.snippet: is said to be definable (or explicitly definable

annotations.note: `Set.Definable A L s` defines definability by a formula with parameters in `A`; the explicit (parameter-free) case is `A = ∅` via `empty_definable_iff`.

anchors.snippet: is said to be definable (or explicitly definable

annotations.label: Definable element

anchor.snippet: An important special case is the definability of specific elements

annotations.note: Definability of elements is handled through `Definable₁`/`Definable.singleton`, but parameter-free definability of a single element is not given its own notion.

anchors.snippet: An important special case is the definability of specific elements

annotations.label: Definable with parameters

anchor.snippet: is said to be definable with parameters

annotations.note: `Set.Definable A L s` is literally definability with parameters drawn from the set `A`.

annotations.label: Every element is definable with itself as parameter

anchor.snippet: Every element of a structure is definable using the element itself as a parameter

annotations.note: `Definable.singleton` proves `{a}` is definable over `{a}`, i.e. each element is definable using itself as a parameter.

annotations.label: Implicit definability

anchor.snippet: is said to be implicitly definable over

annotations.note: Implicit definability of a relation is not defined in Mathlib's ModelTheory.

annotations.label: Beth's theorem

anchor.snippet: By Beth's theorem , every implicitly definable relation is explicitly definable

annotations.note: Beth's definability theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Many-sorted structure

anchor.snippet: A many-sorted structure can have an arbitrary number of domains

annotations.note: Mathlib's first-order model theory is single-sorted; many-sorted structures are not formalized.

annotations.label: Vector spaces as two-sorted structures

anchor.snippet: can be regarded as two-sorted structures in the following way

annotations.note: There is no two-sorted model-theoretic treatment of vector spaces in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Groups: elementary class but not a variety

anchor.snippet: is an elementary class , but it is not a variety

annotations.note: Mathlib has no first-order theory of groups nor the notions of elementary class versus variety in model theory.

annotations.label: Partial functions / partial algebra

anchor.snippet: one is naturally led to allow partial functions, i.e., functions that are defined only on a subset of their domain

annotations.note: Model-theoretic structures use total `funMap`; partial algebras are not formalized in ModelTheory.

annotations.label: Inductively defined types

anchor.snippet: Types are inductively defined

annotations.note: The inductive type hierarchy for typed object languages is not formalized as a model-theoretic notion.

annotations.label: Structure for a typed language

anchor.snippet: must include a separate set of objects of each type

annotations.note: Typed-language structures with a separate domain per type are not formalized in Mathlib's ModelTheory.

annotations.label: Structure under full higher-order semantics

anchor.snippet: a structure need only have a universe for objects of type 0

annotations.note: Higher-order structures with full semantics are not formalized in Mathlib's model theory.

annotations.label: Class models

anchor.snippet: These structures are sometimes called class models

annotations.note: Mathlib structures are over a `Type`, so proper-class models are not formalized.
